glossary.definition: a clustering method that starts with each data object in its own cluster and recursively merges the two clusters with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity at each level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired
glossary.sentence: Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster. This produces a grouping at the next higher level with one less cluster. The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) . Therefore, a pair of clusters is merged at each hierarchy level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level.

glossary.definition: a process that builds a hierarchy of clusters characterised by clusters at each level being created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is about building a hierarchy of clusters that is characterised by the following aspects (Hastie, 2017) : the clusters at each level of the hierarchy are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level

glossary.definition: a clustering method that starts with a cluster containing all data objects and recursively splits one existing cluster into two new clusters at each level, choosing the split that produces two groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters. The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: methods used to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: a linkage method where the distance between clusters is calculated as the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance)
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: a linkage method where the distance between clusters is calculated as the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance)
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: a linkage method where the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: an output of hierarchical clustering that is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset

glossary.definition: a node in a dendrogram representing the merging of data objects into clusters, with each clade having a height that indicates the cluster similarity
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: a terminal node in a dendrogram corresponding to a data object used in the clustering process
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: a line drawn through a dendrogram to determine the number of clusters by counting the number of vertical lines intersected
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: the dissimilarity between two clusters, used to determine which pairs of clusters to merge in agglomerative hierarchical clustering

glossary.definition: the dissimilarity between two groups of data objects, used in divisive hierarchical clustering to determine the split that produces two groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity
glossary.sentence: The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: the height at which two data objects are merged in a dendrogram, serving as an indicator of cluster similarity

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates the fraction of predicted positives that are true positives; the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) . Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates the fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects; the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. According to (Kubat, 2017) , recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: The harmonic mean of precision and recall; a metric that represents the balance between precision and recall in classification tasks.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A curve that plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of a hyperparameter, used to visualize the tradeoff between false positives and true positives.
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff (Russell & Norvig, 2010; Geron, 2017; Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: The area under the ROC curve; a single-number summary that measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the ROC curve, ranging from 0 to 1 where 1 indicates perfect classification and 0.5 indicates random chance.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010). It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve. It returns a value in the range [0, 1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1; in contrast, a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5 (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: Data objects that are correctly predicted as positive in a classification task.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives.

glossary.definition: Data objects that are incorrectly predicted as positive in a classification task.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives.

glossary.definition: Data objects that are correctly classified as negative but should have been classified as positive in a classification task.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification, we first calculate a recall for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives.

glossary.definition: The average precision calculated across all classes in a multiclass classification task.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives. After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.

glossary.definition: The average recall calculated across all classes in a multiclass classification task.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification, we first calculate a recall for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives. After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.term: rule-based expert systems advantages: Uniform structure

glossary.sentence: Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge

glossary.term: rule-based expert systems advantages: Separation of knowledge and control
glossary.definition: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine. glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine

glossary.term: rule-based expert systems advantages: Modularity of production rules
glossary.definition: Rules have no syntactic interactions. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not "call" another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules. This syntactic independence supports the incremental development of expert systems by successively adding, deleting, or modifying the knowledge (rules) of the system
glossary.sentence: Rules have no syntactic interactions

glossary.term: rule-based expert systems advantages: Tracing and explanation
glossary.definition: The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules. At each stage of the control cycle "Recognize-act", the selected rule can be displayed. In addition, because each rule corresponds to a single "chunk" of problem-solving knowledge, the rule content can provide a meaningful explanation of the system’s current state and action
glossary.sentence: The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules

glossary.term: rule-based expert systems advantages: Dealing with incomplete and uncertain knowledge

glossary.sentence: to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one
